message.group-title: Endocrinology (including Diabetes Mellitus and Metabolic Disease)
message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Reports indicate that COVID-19 may impact pancreatic function and increase type 2 diabetes (T2D) risk, although real-world COVID-19 impacts on HbA1c and T2D are unknown. We tested whether COVID-19 increased HbA1c, risk of T2D, or diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We compared pre- and post-COVID-19 HbA1c, and risk of developing T2D in a large real-world clinical cohort of 8,755 COVID-19(+) patients and a matched control cohort of 11,998 COVID-19(-) patients. We investigated if DKA risk was modified in COVID-19(+) patients with type 1 diabetes (T1D) (N=704) or T2D (N=22,904), or by race and sex.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We observed a statistically significant, albeit clinically insignificant, HbA1c increase post-COVID-19 (all patients ΔHbA1c=0.06%,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <.001; with T2D ΔHbA1c=0.1%;
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <.001), and no increase among COVID-19(-) patients (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    >.05). COVID-19(+) patients were 40% more likely to be diagnosed with T2D compared to COVID-19(-) patients (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <.001) and 28% more likely to be diagnosed with T2D for the same HbA1c change as COVID-19(-) patients (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <.001). COVID-19(+) patients with T2D on insulin were 34% more likely to develop DKA compared to COVID-19(-) patients on insulin (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <.05), and COVID-19(+) Black patients with T2D displayed disproportionately increased DKA risk (HR:1.63,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =.007). There was no significant difference in DKA risk between COVID-19(+) and COVID-19(-) patients with T1D.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interpretation</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>DKA risk is increased in T2D patients on insulin and in Black patients with T2D after COVID-19 infection.T2D risk is greater in COVID-19(+) patients for the same HbA1c increase in COVID-19(-) patients, indicating that T2D risk attributed to COVID-19 may be due to increased recognition during COVID-19 management.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>No funding to report.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Impacts of COVID-19 on glycemia and risk of diabetic ketoacidosis
